q: State Epictetus's core principle about what is and is not 'up to us', with a verbatim quote from the corpus.
final:
The core principle of Epictetus regarding what is and is not "up to us" is the dichotomy of control. This principle divides all existence into two categories: those things that are within our power and those that are not.

According to Epictetus:

*   **Things within our power** are our judgments, impulses, desires, aversions, and in short, all that is our own doing. These are naturally free and unhindered.
*   **Things not within our power** include our body, property, reputation, offices, and in short, all that is not our own doing. These are weak, subject to restraint, and dependent on others.

The corpus states: "Of things some are in our power, and others are not. In our power are opinion ([Greek: hupolaepsis]), movement towards a thing ([Greek: hormae]), desire, aversion ([Greek: echchlisis]), turning from a thing; and in a word, whatever are our acts. Not in our power are the body, property, reputation, offices (magisterial power), and in a word, whatever are not our own acts."

tool_calls.name: federated_note_html
tool_calls.result_preview:
Дихотомия контроля (τὰ ἐφ' ἡμῖν)

Первое и главное деление всей системы, открывающее Мануал. Мир разрезан надвое:
то, что в нашей власти (суждение, побуждение, желание, уклонение — «наши
собственные действия»), и то, что не в нашей власти (тело, имущество,
репутация, должности). Первое по природе свободно и не встречает препятствий;
второе слабо, рабственно, подвластно другим.
